topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notice was taped to my door, a stark white square against the faded paint. Inside, nestled in bubble wrap, was a box addressed to 'Apartment 3B.' My stomach clenched. I had been meticulous about the handover, ensuring everything was cleared out, every trace of my presence erased. Now, this arrived. I stared at the label, my jaw tightening. It was clearly meant for the folks who had packed up last month. I picked up the package, the cardboard cool beneath my fingers, and carried it to the curb. I didn't want to open it. I just wanted it gone, a misplaced item from someone else’s life. I walked back to my door, the weight of the unfamiliar object heavy in my hand, and placed it firmly on the welcome mat, a silent declaration that this was not my concern.

[story 2]

I watched the courier leave, the sound of the truck fading into the afternoon hum. The package sat on my kitchen counter, an unwelcome intrusion. It was clearly addressed to the previous tenants, a ghost from a life I was trying to build anew. A slow, tight knot formed in my chest, a feeling of absolute refusal. I circled the counter twice, my gaze sweeping over the item as if it were contaminated. I didn't touch it. I went about my evening routine, the sound of the refrigerator humming a monotonous counterpoint to the internal resistance I felt. Every instinct screamed at me to dispose of it immediately, to sweep it into the bin and forget it existed. But I just stood there, rooted, letting the object sit there, a tangible reminder of a past arrangement I was determined to ignore.

[story 3]

When the doorbell rang, I hesitated before opening it. It was a package. The recipient address was clearly wrong—it was for Unit 3B. A flicker of annoyance sparked in my eyes. I took the box from the hall table, the paper crinkling loudly under my grip. I walked into the living room, placing the package on the coffee table with deliberate care, making sure it was visible. I didn't immediately examine the contents. Instead, I paced the room, my footsteps sharp on the hardwood floor, my shoulders slightly hunched. I kept glancing at the box, a rigid set to my jaw. If this was someone else's problem, it was not mine to solve. I decided, with a firm settling in my core, that I would address it later, perhaps tomorrow, but not now.
